content:
Below is the message log from #mm channel in oftc, I asked about the alignment guarantee of kmalloc API, and got some suggestion:

vbabka is Vlastimil Babka
dhansen is Dave Hansen
willy is Matthew Wilcox

IRC log

2022-09-20 12:31:35 boqun Will we have a kmalloc()-like API that has an "alignment" argument? I ask because Rust's alloc function takes a Layou which is basically a (size, alignment) tuple, if it directly calls kmalloc() or krealloc(), it's not semantically correct
2022-09-20 12:32:12 boqun Some dicussion here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/815#discussion_r914031172
2022-09-20 12:34:50 boqun What Rust-for-Linux can do is the allocating size+alignment-1 trick itself, but that's suboptimal..
2022-09-20 12:37:19 willy slab already guarantees natural alignment for 512-byte and above
2022-09-20 12:38:36 boqun but for example, kmalloc(192) will align to 64 or not?
2022-09-20 12:41:24 dhansen $ cat /sys/kernel/slab/kmalloc-192/align
2022-09-20 12:41:24 dhansen 8
2022-09-20 12:45:02 boqun Does the sl*b implementation take (or will take) advantage of this alignment requirement?
2022-09-20 12:45:45 boqun To save memory I suppose?
2022-09-20 12:48:01 dhansen Are you asking if it will actually align to 8 bytes in practice? Or whether it will just end up doing 64 in practice?
2022-09-20 12:50:08 boqun Both, but I'm more interested in the latter one, i.e. is it in fact 64byte aligned? And if so do we want users to rely on it?
2022-09-20 12:50:15 willy i suspect it wouldn't hurt us (if debugging turned off) to guarantee higher alignment for a lot of general-purpose slabs
2022-09-20 12:50:28 vbabka willy: really 512 and above? IIRC it was for all power-of-2 requests
2022-09-20 12:50:55 willy i'd defer to the slab maintainer, but 512-and-above-powers-of-two was the request from the FS people
2022-09-20 12:51:59 vbabka it made sense just to do it for everything
2022-09-20 12:52:14 vbabka but no 64byte guarantees for 192byte allocations
2022-09-20 12:52:41 boqun To be clear, I'm not asking to butter Rust up, just want to know what we already have ;-)
2022-09-20 12:53:03 vbabka there it will just happen to be alighned if no debugging enabled, but for power-of-two we guarantee even with debugging enabled
2022-09-20 12:53:34 dhansen boqun: it's not guaranteed to remain the same, but you can see what happens in practice pretty easily
2022-09-20 12:53:37 dhansen echo 1 > /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/events/kmem/kmalloc/enable; cat /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/trace_pipe | grep bytes_req =192
2022-09-20 12:53:57 boqun so that Rust can do things like: if CONFIG_XXX=y, alloc(192) is 64 aligned, just do the allocation, otherwise fallback to size+align-1 approach
2022-09-20 12:54:14 dhansen oh and you need to disable /sys/kernel/debug/tracing/options/hash-ptr
2022-09-20 12:54:56 vbabka debugging is runtime enabled so CONFIG won't be enough
2022-09-20 12:55:22 vbabka more precisely, boot-time enabled
2022-09-20 12:56:12 dhansen boqun: honestly, for now, I'd just stick with the "fallback"
2022-09-20 12:56:22 dhansen it penalizes rust code only so it won't rock the boat
2022-09-20 12:56:27 boqun vbabka: Got it
2022-09-20 12:56:38 boqun dhansen: fine by me
2022-09-20 12:57:31 <-- chipxxx (~chip@pop.92-184-96-137.mobile.abo.orange.fr) has quit (Read error: Connection reset by peer)
2022-09-20 12:57:51 vbabka the fallback of size+align-1 sucks, it would need to track the pointer returned by kmalloc() that has to be passed to kfree(), and then the pointer used internally?
2022-09-20 13:00:03 willy hrm, i thought kfree(kmalloc(x, GFP_*) + 1) worked
2022-09-20 13:00:34 willy i don't think we guarantee it, but it'd be OK for the rust runtime
2022-09-20 13:00:46 vbabka just calculate sufficient power-of-two size that gives you the needed alignment and fits the object
2022-09-20 13:01:08 vbabka in the github link I see "there are no ways that we can do allocation for a layout, say, with size = 192 and alignment = 64."
2022-09-20 13:01:18 vbabka so that's a kmalloc(256) and you can use the pointer directly
2022-09-20 13:01:53 vbabka willy: SLOB would explode and the debugging code in SLAB/SLUB would also complain I think
2022-09-20 13:02:43 boqun vbabka: that's just a wrong comment (the "no ways" one) ;-)
2022-09-20 13:02:45 willy pretty sure we have permission to delete SLOB now ;-)
2022-09-20 13:03:21 willy anyway, this is feeling dangerously like work. going to put the laptop down now and go back to being on holiday
2022-09-20 13:04:12 boqun vbabka: calculating sufficient power-of-two size does seems better
2022-09-20 13:04:31 vbabka willy: enjoy
2022-09-20 13:04:41 vbabka looks like I'm the only one who didn't stay in Ireland or what
2022-09-20 13:05:06 boqun I got back to work at Sep 15 ;-(
2022-09-20 13:07:37 boqun so for align < 512, we calculate the sufficient power-of-two, and use that as the size of kmalloc(), for align >=512, we just allocate with the size (size is always a multiple of the alignment). Sounds reasonable?
2022-09-20 13:08:19 boqun I assume that kmalloc(1536) gives me a 512 aligned memory
2022-09-20 13:14:10 vbabka well that's because we happen to have caches sized 1024 and 2048 and nothing in between
2022-09-20 13:14:22 vbabka but there's no guarantee
2022-09-20 13:14:26 vbabka only for power-of-two
2022-09-20 13:15:53 vbabka but the only way to change it is to modify the kernel source so you could say that whoever does that needs to fix up Rust's layer too

One of the suggestion I got from vbabka is

2022-09-20 13:00:46 vbabka just calculate sufficient power-of-two size that gives you the needed alignment and fits the object

I'm not 100% sure that's good, but put the log here.
Ideally we can have a kernel API that allows custom alignment.
